citation_label: Repaying your student loan
heading: Repaying your student loan

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan

body:
You need to pay back:

Tuition Fee Loans

Maintenance Loans for living costs

postgraduate loans - for example, a Master’s Loan or Doctoral Loan

You do not need to pay back other student finance, for example grants and bursaries. You will have to repay any overpayments if you receive more of any type of student finance than you’re entitled to.

You still have to repay your student loan if you leave your course early .

This guide is also available in Welsh (Cymraeg) .

When you start repaying your loan and how much you pay depends on which repayment plan you’re on .

Keep your contact details up to date in your online account so that you can get messages about your loan.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan 1
heading: Overview

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan

body:
How you repay your loan depends on whether you’re:

employed

self-employed or need to complete a Self Assessment tax return for another reason

You can make extra repayments in your online account and by card, bank transfer or cheque.

Keep your payslips and your P60 for your records - you’ll need them if you want to get a refund .
citation_label: Repaying your student loan 2
heading: How to repay

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan

body:
You must tell the Student Loans Company (SLC) if you’re leaving the UK for more than 3 months - for example, if you go travelling or if you move overseas.

Tell SLC by updating your employment details .

You’ll be expected to keep repaying your loan unless you can give proof (for example, a recent bank statement) that your overseas income is below the threshold.

If you do not tell SLC, you could build up debt (‘accrue arrears’) on your account . You’ll need to pay arrears back on top of your regular repayments.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan 3
heading: If you leave the UK for more than 3 months

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan

body:
You must update your employment details when you return to the UK after more than 3 months away.

If you do not, you’ll continue repaying your loan at the rate for the country you’ve been living in. That could mean:

paying more than you need to

you’re charged a higher rate of interest
citation_label: Repaying your student loan 4
heading: If you return to the UK after more than 3 months away

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan

body: Sign in to your online account if you already have one.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan 5
heading: Sign in

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan

body:
Step 1 : Get your National Insurance number You'll usually need your National Insurance number when you start your new job. Find your National Insurance number Apply for a National Insurance number if you do not already have one

Step 2 : Accept the job offer The offer might be ‘conditional’ or ‘provisional’, which means you may need to give further details to your new employer or pass pre-employment checks. Find out about your rights when accepting a job offer

Step 3 : Share your details for any employer checks Give your new employer what they need to complete any pre-employment checks. Find out what checks your employer can make Find out how to prove your right to work Find out about criminal record checks

Step 4 : Review your employment contract Your new employer should give you an employment contract. Check the terms before you agree to it. Find out about employment contracts

and If you're leaving a job, hand in your notice Tell your current employer that you’re going to leave and when your last day will be. This is known as handing in your notice. Find out how to hand in your notice When you hand in your notice, ask your employer when you’ll get your P45. Find out about the P45

Step 5 : Prepare your personal information for your first day To get paid, you’ll need to give your new employer: your National Insurance number your bank details proof of your identity what student loan repayment plan you’re on, if you have one details of what benefits you get, if any

Step 6 : Give your P45 to your new employer Your new employer will use your P45 to work out how much tax to take from your pay. Find out about the P45

or If you do not have a P45, fill in the starter checklist Use the starter checklist to give your employer details they need to work out your pay and tax. Fill in the starter checklist

Step 7 : Check your first payslip Your employer must give you a payslip on or before payday. Find out what should be on your payslip It’s your responsibility to make sure you’re paying the right amount of tax. Check if the tax on your payslip is correct If you think the tax is wrong, your tax code may be incorrect. Find out what to do if you think your tax code is wrong
citation_label: Repaying your student loan 6
heading: This page is Part of Start a new job: step by step

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan

citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay
heading: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body:
How much you repay depends on your income - the amount you earn (including things like bonuses and overtime) before tax and other deductions.

The amount you owe (which is a total of what you’ve borrowed plus interest) has no impact on how much you repay each year.

You’ll repay a percentage of your income over the income threshold for your type of loan.

The income thresholds are different for each plan type, and depend on how often you get paid.

Plan type Yearly income threshold Monthly income threshold Weekly income threshold Plan 1 £26,900 £2,241 £517 Plan 2 £29,385 £2,448 £565 Plan 4 £33,795 £2,816 £649 Plan 5 £25,000 £2,083 £480 Postgraduate Loan £21,000 £1,750 £403

You’ll repay either:

9% of your income over the threshold if you’re on Plan 1, 2, 4 or 5

6% of your income over the threshold if you’re on a Postgraduate Loan plan

The examples show how much you’d repay depending on your income and plan type:

Example

You’re on Plan 1 and have an income of £33,000 a year, meaning you get paid £2,750 each month.

Calculation:

£2,750 – £2,241 (your income minus the Plan 1 threshold) = £509

9% of £509 = £45.81

This means the amount you’d repay each month would be £45.

Example

You’re on Plan 4 and have an income of £36,000 a year, meaning you get paid £3,000 each month.

Calculation:

£3,000 – £2,816 (your income minus the Plan 4 threshold) = £184

9% of £184 = £16.56

This means the amount you’d repay each month would be £16.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 1
heading: How much you repay

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body: You’ll make a repayment if your income goes over the weekly or monthly threshold for your plan (for example, if you’re paid a bonus or overtime). You can ask for a refund at the end of the tax year if your annual income is less than the yearly threshold for your plan.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 2
heading: If your income changes during the year

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body:
How much interest you’re charged depends on which plan you’re on. You’re currently charged:

3.2% if you’re on Plan 1

3.2% if you’re on Plan 4

3.2% if you’re on Plan 5

6.2% if you’re on a Postgraduate Loan plan

Interest is still added (‘applied’) to the amount you owe even if you’re not working or your income is below the repayment threshold.

You can see how interest has been applied in your annual statement. This is normally available in your online repayment account by the end of August.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 3
heading: Interest

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body: Plan 2 interest is a Variable Interest Rate (VIR). This means the interest you’re charged can change depending on your circumstances.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 4
heading: Interest if you’re on Plan 2

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body:
You’re charged a set interest rate of the Retail Price Index (RPI) plus 3%. You’re charged this rate from the day your first payment is made to you, or to your university or college.

The RPI plus 3% is currently 6.2%.

The interest rate you’re charged will change either:

on 6 April after you finish or leave your course

on 6 April 4 years after the course started, if you’re studying part-time and your course is longer than 4 years
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 5
heading: While you’re studying

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body:
You’ll be charged a Variable Interest Rate based on your annual income. This is the Retail Price Index plus up to 3%.

Annual income Interest rate £29,385 or less 3.2% £29,386 to £52,884 3.2%, plus up to 3% £52,885 or more 6.2%

You can find out more about:

how Plan 1 interest is calculated and interest rates for previous years

how Plan 2 interest is calculated and interest rates for previous years

how Plan 4 interest is calculated and interest rates for previous years

how Plan 5 interest is calculated and interest rates for previous years

how Postgraduate Loan interest is calculated and interest rates for previous years
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 6
heading: After you finish or leave your course

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body:
You’ll repay 9% of your income over the lowest threshold out of the plan types you have.

You’ll only have a single repayment taken each time you get paid, even if you’re paying towards more than one loan.

Example

You’re on Plan 1 and Plan 2. You have an income of £38,400 a year, meaning you get paid £3,200 a month.

£3,200 is over the Plan 1 monthly threshold of £2,241 and the Plan 2 monthly threshold of £2,448.

You’ll repay 9% of your income over £2,241 a month because that’s the lowest threshold out of the plan types you have.

Calculation:

£3,200 - £2,241 (your income minus the lowest threshold) = £959

9% of £959 = £86.31

This means the amount you’d repay each month would be £86.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 7
heading: If you’re on more than one plan type and do not have a Postgraduate Loan

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body:
There is a limit (or ‘cap’) to the amount you can repay each month towards the plan type with the lowest threshold.

The difference between the thresholds for each plan determines what the cap will be.

If you go over the cap, the rest of your repayment will go towards your other loans.

Example

You’re on Plan 1 and Plan 2. You repay £86 per month.

The cap will apply to your Plan 1 loan because it has the lowest threshold. Anything you repay over the cap will then go towards your Plan 2 loan.

Calculation:

£2,448 - £2,241 (Plan 2 threshold minus Plan 1 threshold) = £207

9% (the Plan 1 rate) of £200 = £18.63

The monthly payment cap for your Plan 1 loan is £18.

£18 of your £86 repayment will go towards your Plan 1 loan and the remaining £68 will go towards your Plan 2 loan.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 8
heading: When your repayment gets split between loans

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body:
You’ll repay 6% of your income over the Postgraduate Loan threshold (£21,000 a year) and 9% of your income over the lowest threshold for any other plan types you have.

Example

You have a Postgraduate Loan and a Plan 2 loan and have an income of £30,000 a year, meaning you get paid £2,500 each month. This is over the Postgraduate Loan threshold of £1,750 and the Plan 2 threshold of £2,448.

Calculation:

£2,500 – £1,750 (your income minus the Postgraduate Loan threshold) = £750

6% of £750 = £45

£2,500 – £2,448 (your income minus the Plan 2 threshold) = £52

9% of £52 = £4.68

This means the amount you’d repay each month would be £49.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 9
heading: If you’re on more than one plan type and have a Postgraduate Loan

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body:
You’ll only make repayments from jobs where you’re paid over the threshold for your plan type, not your combined income.

Example

You have a Plan 1 loan and you have 2 jobs. Before tax and other deductions, you’re paid £1,000 a month from one job and £800 a month for the other.

You will not have to make repayments because neither salary is above the £2,241 a month threshold.

Example

You have a Plan 2 loan and you have 2 jobs. Before tax and other deductions, you’re paid £2,500 a month from one job and £500 a month for the other.

You will only make repayments on the income from the job that pays you £2,500 a month because it’s above the £2,448 threshold.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 10
heading: If you’re employed and have more than one employer

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body: HM Revenue and Customs ( HMRC ) will work out how much you repay each year from your Self Assessment tax return. Your repayments will be based on your income for the whole year.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 11
heading: If you’re self-employed or complete a Self Assessment tax return for another reason

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body:
How much you repay depends on your combined income for the whole year.

Example

You have a Plan 1 loan and you’re both employed and self-employed. You have an income of £32,000 a year from your self-employment and £10,000 a year from a job with an employer.

Your Self Assessment tax return will show that your combined income is £42,000. This is over the yearly threshold for Plan 1.

Calculation:

£42,000 - £26,900 (your income minus the Plan 1 threshold) = £15,100

9% of £15,100 = £1,359

This means the amount you’d repay each year would be £1,359.

If you’ve already made repayments from a salary, HMRC will deduct them from the amount you have to repay.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 12
heading: If you’re also employed

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body:
Step 1 : Get your National Insurance number You'll usually need your National Insurance number when you start your new job. Find your National Insurance number Apply for a National Insurance number if you do not already have one

Step 2 : Accept the job offer The offer might be ‘conditional’ or ‘provisional’, which means you may need to give further details to your new employer or pass pre-employment checks. Find out about your rights when accepting a job offer

Step 3 : Share your details for any employer checks Give your new employer what they need to complete any pre-employment checks. Find out what checks your employer can make Find out how to prove your right to work Find out about criminal record checks

Step 4 : Review your employment contract Your new employer should give you an employment contract. Check the terms before you agree to it. Find out about employment contracts

and If you're leaving a job, hand in your notice Tell your current employer that you’re going to leave and when your last day will be. This is known as handing in your notice. Find out how to hand in your notice When you hand in your notice, ask your employer when you’ll get your P45. Find out about the P45

Step 5 : Prepare your personal information for your first day To get paid, you’ll need to give your new employer: your National Insurance number your bank details proof of your identity what student loan repayment plan you’re on, if you have one details of what benefits you get, if any

Step 6 : Give your P45 to your new employer Your new employer will use your P45 to work out how much tax to take from your pay. Find out about the P45

or If you do not have a P45, fill in the starter checklist Use the starter checklist to give your employer details they need to work out your pay and tax. Fill in the starter checklist

Step 7 : Check your first payslip Your employer must give you a payslip on or before payday. Find out what should be on your payslip It’s your responsibility to make sure you’re paying the right amount of tax. Check if the tax on your payslip is correct If you think the tax is wrong, your tax code may be incorrect. Find out what to do if you think your tax code is wrong
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: What you pay 13
heading: This page is Part of Start a new job: step by step

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

citation_label: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund
heading: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund

body:
You can ask for a refund if:

you’ve paid more than the total amount you owe

your annual income was below the threshold

you started making repayments before you needed to

you’ve repaid more than you need to because your employer had you on the wrong repayment plan

You cannot get a refund for extra repayments.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund 1
heading: Getting a refund

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund

body:
HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) will tell your employer to stop taking repayments from your salary when you have repaid your loan in full. It can take around 4 weeks for salary deductions to stop.

This means you may pay back more than you owe.

You can avoid paying more than you owe by changing your payments to Direct Debit in the final year of your repayments. Keep your contact details up to date in your online account so SLC can let you know how to set this up.

If you have paid too much the Student Loans Company ( SLC ) will try to:

contact you to tell you how to get a refund

refund you automatically (this will appear in your bank account as ‘ SLC Receipts’)

You can check your loan balance in your online account .

If you’ve overpaid and have not heard from SLC you can ask them for a refund.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund 2
heading: If you pay back more than you owe

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund

body:
Your income is the amount you earn (including things like bonuses and overtime) before tax and other deductions.

You can ask for a refund if you made repayments but your income over the whole tax year (6 April to 5 April the following year) was less than:

£26,900 a year for Plan 1

£29,385 a year for Plan 2

£33,795 a year for Plan 4

£21,000 a year for Postgraduate Loan

If your annual salary is less than this, your employer may still have deducted repayments. For example, if you were paid a bonus that put you over the monthly threshold for your plan, they would deduct a repayment for that month.

You will not get a refund for any repayments until after SLC have confirmed your annual income with HMRC. This will not happen until after the end of the tax year. You can only ask for a refund for tax years that have ended.

If you’re repaying a combination of Plan 1, Plan 2 and Plan 4 loans, you can only get a refund if your income was less than the lowest threshold.

Example

You’re repaying a Plan 1 and Plan 2 loan.

The Plan 1 threshold is £26,900 a year.

The Plan 2 threshold is £29,385 a year.

The Plan 1 threshold is lower.

This means you can only get a refund if your income was less than the Plan 1 threshold (£26,900 a year).

Check which repayment plan you’re on if you’re not sure.

You can check previous thresholds if you ask about a refund on repayments made before this tax year.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund 3
heading: If your annual income was below the threshold

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund

body: If a deduction is taken from your salary before you’re due to start repaying , you can ask for a refund.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund 4
heading: If you started repaying before you needed to

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund

body: You can get a refund if you’re on Plan 2 or Plan 4 but your employer had you on another plan. Check your employer has you on the correct repayment plan .
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund 5
heading: If your employer had you on the wrong repayment plan

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund

body: Sign into your student loan repayment account and ask for a refund.
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund 6
heading: If your annual income was below the threshold for the previous tax year

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund

body: Contact the Student Loans Company with your customer reference number ( CRN ).
citation_label: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund 7
heading: If you need a refund for any other reason
